reasoning: The goal to map a >1 exabyte connectome (specifically the whole mouse brain) by June 2031 is currently expected to be technologically feasible but faces funding headwinds. In 2021, a 1mm³ human brain sample produced a 1.4-petabyte map, showing that a 1000mm³ whole mouse brain would yield ~1.4 exabytes. Researchers like Bobby Kasthuri (Argonne/UChicago) have previously projected completing the mouse connectome within five years of the fly connectome (which was published in 2024), pointing to a 2029 finish line. Argonne’s Aurora exascale supercomputer is actively being prepped for this massive dataset. However, a major 2023 Wellcome Trust report estimated a whole mouse connectome on a 10-15 year timeline (2033+). Furthermore, significant funding cuts to the NIH BRAIN Initiative ($278M in late 2024) and the cancellation of specific Harvard multi-site connectome scaling grants in 2025 demonstrate that purely public U.S. efforts may be delayed. Despite this, private sector integration (like Google Research's continued computational resourcing) and robust computational advances keep the project viable. The community estimate in April 2026 stood at 0.58, reflecting resilience despite the 2024-2025 funding disruptions. My estimate is aligned slightly below the community average at 0.55-0.58, balancing the high capabilities of the engaged institutions against realistic hardware/funding timelines.
